data.title: Floor & Decor outlines 2026 EPS of $1.83-$2.08 while launching $400M buyback
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Floor & Decor (FND) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Bradley Paulsen (President, CEO & Director) centered the quarter on a new capital return move, saying the board “has authorized a share repurchase program for up to $400 million” and adding, “We do not expect to use incremental debt to support the share repurchase program, and there is no defined time line for the share repurchase program's completion.”
  • Paulsen attributed weaker-than-expected Q1 demand to multiple pressures and said, “These dynamics resulted in first quarter earnings coming in weaker than we anticipated,” while highlighting category and regional dispersion and noting, “Our second quarter-to-date comparable store sales declined 4.5%.”
  • Paulsen emphasized actions in vinyl price architecture, saying, “Although still early, results from our price band refinements are encouraging with positive elasticity and improving square footage purchase trends,” while also cautioning, “we do expect the category to be under pressure for the remainder of 2026.”
  • Bryan Langley (Executive VP, CFO & Principal Accounting Officer) highlighted margin execution and ERP progress, stating, “We continue to effectively manage our gross margin with our first quarter performance exceeding our expectations,” and “we successfully completed portions of our ERP implementation and are now live with financial systems and certain merchandising portions in the first quarter.”

Outlook

  • Langley reframed guidance around heightened uncertainty, saying, “Given the unexpected events that emerged following our fourth quarter earnings release, we believe it is prudent to reflect a wider range of potential outcomes in our fiscal 2026 guidance.”
  • Langley guided fiscal 2026 sales to $4.770 billion to $4.990 billion and comparable store sales to flat to down 4%, and said, “Diluted earnings per share is estimated to be approximately $1.83 to $2.08,” while adding, “The first quarter gross margin of 44.0% is likely to represent a high point for the year.”
  • Versus the prior quarter’s guidance, management lowered the full-year sales range (previously $4.880 billion to $5.03 billion), lowered the comp range (previously down 2% to up 1%), and lowered the diluted EPS range (previously $1.98 to $2.18), while keeping CapEx unchanged at $250 million to $300 million.

Financial Results

  • Paulsen reported Q1 diluted EPS of $0.37, sales of $1.152 billion, and comparable store sales down 3.7%, and said the comp decline was “driven by a 5.5% decrease in transactions,” partially offset by “a 1.9% increase in average ticket.”
  • Langley reported gross margin of 44.0% and said SG&A as a percentage of sales was 39.5%, while operating income was $52.4 million and adjusted EBITDA was $121.5 million.
  • Langley said operating cash flow was $109.2 million and ended Q1 with $1,007.2 million in unrestricted liquidity, consisting of $293.6 million of cash and cash equivalents and $713.6 million available under the ABL Facility.

Q&A

  • Seth Sigman, Barclays: Asked what’s driving laminate/vinyl weakness; President Paulsen: Said “it’s really the vinyl part,” tied to a “shift in consumer preference” to “sub-$2,” and added, “our mindset is we're going to take share.”
  • Seth Sigman, Barclays: Asked about EPS sensitivity and cost offsets; Executive VP Langley: Said higher energy costs are “embedded,” and cited labor flex, discretionary spend, and DC/G&A management.
  • Simeon Gutman, Morgan Stanley: Asked why not slow store openings and how it relates to buybacks; President Paulsen: Said “we remain committed to 20 stores”; Executive VP Langley: Said average store cost is “approximately $7.5 million to $8 million” vs. “$11.7 million in 2023.”
  • Simeon Gutman, Morgan Stanley: Asked about lower-end value positioning; President Paulsen: Said “better, best” has been “resilient,” with laminate/vinyl as “the only exception.”
  • Steven Forbes, Guggenheim: Asked how 55,000 sq. ft. stores preserve productivity; President Paulsen: Said “we don't believe that we're going to sacrifice any type of top line productivity,” and tied the shift to densifying urban markets.
  • Steven Zaccone, Citi: Asked why lower guidance so early; President Paulsen: Said it was “prudent and responsible…to recalibrate,” and described a “wider” range due to uncertainty.
  • Michael Lasser, UBS: Challenged market share readings; President Paulsen: Responded, “We don't see that we're losing share,” referencing “publicly available data,” third-party sources, and vendor feedback.
  • Michael Lasser, UBS: Asked to quantify pricing tests and why not raise prices more; President Paulsen: Said pricing behavior is “rational,” described testing price bands, and emphasized “taking market share.”
  • Zachary Fadem, Wells Fargo: Asked about freight/PVC and Pro loyalty mechanics; Executive VP Langley: Described ocean renegotiations in “spring and early summer” with “6 to 8 months” P&L lag; President Paulsen: Said loyalty is led by “Krysta Zell, our new Chief Customer Officer,” but details aren’t ready.
  • David Bellinger, Mizuho: Asked buyback aggressiveness; Executive VP Langley: Said it is “discretionary,” and “We do plan to start executing in the second quarter.”
  • Charles Grom, Gordon Haskett: Asked about independents and cadence; President Paulsen: Said many independents are “struggling” and reiterated an “offense” posture; Executive VP Langley: Discussed transaction-driven variability and quarterly cadence assumptions.
  • Peter Keith, Piper Sandler: Asked about big box competition; President Paulsen: Said, “we don't see that,” and added, “in some cases, them leaning out of the category.”
  • Katharine McShane, Goldman Sachs: Asked about Spartan timing; President Paulsen: Said Q1 was “even softer than we expected,” but cited “rising quoting activity,” “stable sample volume,” and expected “gradual improvement.”
  • Jonathan Matuszewski, Jefferies: Asked whether installation stance is changing; President Paulsen: Said, “no…we're not going to deviate from the current strategy around installation services.”
  • Phillip Blee, William Blair: Asked about designer loyalty expansion; President Paulsen: Said it is “an opportunity that we can lean into more.”
  • Gregory Melich, Evercore ISI: Asked about pricing actions and ticket; Executive VP Langley: Declined to quantify and tied the low-end ticket outlook to laminate/vinyl pressure.
  • Peter Benedict, Baird: Asked if buybacks are assumed and minimum liquidity; Executive VP Langley: Said impact “isn't going to be very material” this year and cited a “floor” of “around $500 million…minimum in liquidity.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to pressing, challenging guidance timing and market share, including “it is very difficult for us to have an accurate assessment of market share” (Michael Lasser, UBS).
  • Management was neutral to slightly positive in prepared remarks (buyback authorization; store expansion confidence) and more defensive in Q&A on share/guidance, including “We don't see that we're losing share” (President Paulsen).
  • Versus last quarter’s more optimistic framing around “broad-based improvement…in January,” the current call introduced more caution via “wider range of potential outcomes” (Executive VP Langley).

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Management added a new shareholder-return action (a $400 million repurchase authorization) and expanded discussion of execution timing (“start executing in the second quarter”).
  • Guidance language shifted from weather-disruption framing last quarter to broader macro/geopolitical and cost-volatility framing, with explicit emphasis on uncertainty and a wider outcomes range.
  • Analysts kept pressure on market share and competition in both quarters, while the current quarter added more detailed probing on buyback mechanics and freight/input cost flow-through.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited continued pressure on “big-ticket discretionary purchases,” elevated mortgage rates, and higher energy/logistics costs, with mitigation focused on pricing initiatives, vendor negotiations, and cost controls (labor hour alignment, DC and discretionary spend tightening).
  • Paulsen flagged ongoing category risk, saying laminate/vinyl (especially vinyl) faces continued pressure, and Spartan faced “ongoing difficult conditions in the commercial market,” particularly multifamily delays.

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as weaker than expected amid macro and category pressures, paired with a major capital allocation pivot via a $400 million share repurchase authorization while reiterating 20 store openings. The company tightened its fiscal 2026 outlook ranges and emphasized cost discipline, pricing tests (notably sub-$2 vinyl/value offers), and continued investment in Pro initiatives (including a Pro loyalty launch targeted for Q1 2027) as its core levers to navigate uncertain demand and pursue market share gains.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: IRhythm forecasts 2026 revenue of $875M-$885M while targeting 12%-13% adjusted EBITDA margin
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: iRhythm (IRTC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We delivered a strong first quarter, exceeding expectations on both the top and bottom line" (President, CEO & Director Quentin Blackford), adding that "Revenue grew 26% year-over-year" and performance was "broad-based across Zio Monitor and Zio AT" with progress across "cardiology, primary care, innovative channels and international."
  • "Approximately 53% of our volume now flows through EHR integrated accounts and more than 3/4 of our top 100 customers are now integrated" (President, CEO & Director Blackford), and he framed primary care as upstream expansion: "This is not a shift away from cardiology. Rather, it expands the overall market."
  • "In the U.K., we had our best quarter in company history" (President, CEO & Director Blackford); in Japan, "we recently received an update to the reimbursement framework that introduces a modest supplemental payment for longer duration monitoring."
  • "We now have more than 3 billion hours of curated ECG data" (President, CEO & Director Blackford), and he said early predictive pilots show "more than 85% accuracy in pre-identifying patients with clinically relevant arrhythmias" as the company starts "our first health system deployment of predictive identification workflows."
  • "We remain subject to an FDA warning letter" (President, CEO & Director Blackford), but he said a third-party quality review "did not identify any material observations" and added: "While the timing of any action by the FDA remains with the agency, we believe the work completed to-date positions us well."
  • "We reaffirm our first half 2027 release time line" for next-generation MCT (President, CEO & Director Blackford), noting FDA process changes: "the preferred path is to provide a complete package once all elements are finalized later this year."
  • "We delivered revenue of $199.4 million" (CFO & Principal Financial Officer Daniel Wilson) and said results included "880 basis points of improvement to adjusted EBITDA margin" to "$14.1 million or 7.1% of revenue."

Outlook

  • "We are raising full-year 2026 revenue guidance to $875 million to $885 million" (CFO & Principal Financial Officer Wilson) and "raising our full-year 2026 adjusted EBITDA margin guidance to 12% to 13%."
  • "In the second quarter of 2026, we anticipate revenue to be in the range of $218 million to $220 million" (CFO & Principal Financial Officer Wilson) vs. $218,151,030 (analystsEstimates).
  • "For the second quarter 2026, we anticipate adjusted EBITDA margin to be between 11.5% and 12.5%" (CFO & Principal Financial Officer Wilson).
  • "We have cost containment initiatives in place and do not expect a material impact to gross margin" from "the current geopolitical situation" (CFO & Principal Financial Officer Wilson).
  • Compared with the prior quarter, iRhythm increased its 2026 revenue outlook from "$870 million to $880 million" to "$875 million to $885 million" (CFO & Principal Financial Officer Wilson, prior transcript vs. current transcript) and lifted adjusted EBITDA margin guidance from "11.5% to 12.5%" to "12% to 13%" (CFO & Principal Financial Officer Wilson, prior vs. current).

Financial Results

  • $199.4 million revenue (CFO & Principal Financial Officer Wilson) vs. $194,092,170 (analystsEstimates).
  • "Adjusted net loss for the first quarter was $11.3 million or a net loss of $0.35 per diluted share" (CFO & Principal Financial Officer Wilson) vs. (-$0.64) (analystsEstimates).
  • "Gross margin in the first quarter was 70.9%" (CFO & Principal Financial Officer Wilson), and he attributed improvement to "manufacturing automation and workflow optimization as well as scale benefits from higher volumes."
  • "Free cash flow during the first quarter was negative $33 million" (CFO & Principal Financial Officer Wilson) and "We ended the quarter with $549.6 million in cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities" (CFO & Principal Financial Officer Wilson).

Q&A

  • Allen Gong, JPMorgan: asked why the raised guide implies "a bit of a deceleration"; CFO Wilson: "It is early in the year" and "the back part of the year... starts to have some pretty difficult comps" while leaving "potential upside" outside the guide; President, CEO & Director Blackford added: "we're encouraged by what we're seeing" in April.
  • Allen Gong, JPMorgan: asked about AI-driven competition; President, CEO & Director Blackford: "we're not simply just offering a software capability or an algorithm" and emphasized "93% of all lives in the U.S. are now covered with respect to access to Zio" plus "98% of our folks will wear the patch up to 14 days."
  • Stephanie Piazzola, BofA: pressed on EBITDA margin outperformance and limited guide raise; CFO Wilson: "we are raising the guide essentially by the magnitude of the beat in the quarter" and pointed to efficiencies, "one-to-many selling model" leverage in innovative channels, and EHR integration.
  • Stephanie Piazzola, BofA: asked about next-gen algorithm timing and rollout; President, CEO & Director Blackford: "We would expect approval later this year" and "We will implement it alongside MCT... in the first half of '27."
  • Max (for Brandon Vazquez), William Blair: asked what was learned in innovative channel; President, CEO & Director Blackford: "every single one of these partners who patched with us in 2025 is up and patching consistently in 2026" and said adoption is expanding from "asymptomatic" toward "symptomatic" use.
  • Kevin (for Vijay Kumar), Evercore ISI: asked about DOJ CID outcomes; President, CEO & Director Blackford: CID requests were "very consistent with the original subpoena" and he said iRhythm would not "speculate on" outcomes.
  • Nathan Treybeck, Wells Fargo: asked about benefits from reconfirmations for chart-derived diagnoses; President, CEO & Director Blackford: "We haven't contemplated anything in the guide" but called it "a nice tailwind."
  • David Rescott, Baird: asked when sleep could matter; President, CEO & Director Blackford: "In terms of meaningful contribution, we'll talk about that as we get out into '27" and positioned sleep as an "end-to-end" workflow play.
  • Alex (for Marie Thibault), BTIG: asked about Japan reimbursement; CFO Wilson: "We did see a small increase" but said improving reimbursement is "likely a 2027 event" tied to head-to-head data.
  • Filipe (for Richard Newitter), Truist: asked about proposed LCD impact; President, CEO & Director Blackford: as written it "would move just about everything into an MCT category" and "would have a significant uplift from a revenue perspective," adding "timing remains uncertain" due to a "quiet period."
  • Suraj Kalia, Oppenheimer: asked for market sizing and EP slowdown; President, CEO & Director Blackford: monitoring market is "around 6.5 million to 7 million tests" with "about 1 million MCT tests" and said, "there's nothing in our data" suggesting an EP slowdown.
  • Gene Mannheimer, Freedom Capital Markets: asked about long-term targets and primary care; CFO Wilson: "We have not updated" long-term guidance and reiterated primary care is "roughly 1/3 or a little bit over 30% of our volume."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to neutral, with repeated pressure on conservatism in guidance, durability of margin upside, and external overhangs (AI competition, DOJ CID, LCD), including, "how concerned are you" (Allen Gong, JPMorgan) and requests for outcome ranges on DOJ (Kevin for Vijay Kumar, Evercore ISI).
  • Management: slightly positive and measured; CFO Wilson used caution language including "It is early in the year" and "we don't like to get ahead of ourselves," while President, CEO & Director Blackford used confidence framing such as "I feel very good" on AI defensibility and "reaffirm" on MCT timing.
  • Versus the prior quarter, management maintained similar confidence on upstream expansion and AI defensibility, while Q1 included more compliance/legal updates in prepared remarks: "With respect to the DOJ... we have not received any request for additional information" (President, CEO & Director Blackford).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from initial 2026 setup to an in-year increase: Q4 set 2026 revenue at "$870 million to $880 million" and Q1 raised it to "$875 million to $885 million" (CFO & Principal Financial Officer Wilson, prior vs. current).
  • Profitability outlook increased: Q4 guided "11.5% to 12.5%" adjusted EBITDA margin for 2026, while Q1 raised to "12% to 13%" (CFO & Principal Financial Officer Wilson, prior vs. current).
  • Regulatory narrative advanced: Q4 emphasized active dialogue on next-gen MCT and gateway changes; Q1 added third-party QMS review completion and stated it "did not identify any material observations" (President, CEO & Director Blackford).
  • Analyst focus remained consistent on AI and channel durability; Q1 questions broadened to proposed LCD mechanics and DOJ CID specifics.

Risks and Concerns

  • "We remain subject to an FDA warning letter" (President, CEO & Director Blackford), with mitigation described as completing a "comprehensive review" and an independent third-party review with no "material observations."
  • "With respect to the DOJ, we have not received any request for additional information since the CID issued in December" (President, CEO & Director Blackford), and he said the company would not "speculate on" outcomes.
  • "Regarding finalization of the local coverage determination... we have not yet heard back from the MACs" (President, CEO & Director Blackford) and said timing is uncertain due to an "official silent period."

Final Takeaway

iRhythm management described Q1 as a volume-driven start with raised 2026 revenue and adjusted EBITDA margin guidance, alongside continued emphasis on EHR-integrated expansion into primary care and innovative channels. Management highlighted progress on FDA remediation work, reaffirmed a first-half 2027 next-gen MCT launch timeline, and positioned a next-generation AI algorithm as a major efficiency lever expected to receive FDA approval later in 2026 and be implemented with MCT in 2027, while noting ongoing uncertainty around DOJ and Medicare coverage policy timing.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Japan’s industrial growth accelerates to 55.1 in April while Tokyo inflation stays below BoJ target
Petrobras hits record output with oil markets rattled by Iran war
data.description:
Petrobras (PBR) reported record oil and gas output in Q1, supported by strong growth in its pre-salt fields and improved refining performance.

The company said total production of oil, natural gas liquids and gas reached an all-time high of 3.23M barrels of oil equivalent per day, rising 16% from a year earlier and 3.7% from the previous quarter.

Petrobras (PBR) also posted broader operational records, with total operated production reaching 4.65M boe/d and output from pre-salt reserves surpassing 4M boe/d.

In downstream, refining performance strengthened, with derivatives production rising 6.7% to about 1.816M barrels per day. Refinery utilization reached 95% during the quarter.

The record performance comes against a backdrop of heightened geopolitical tensions, with the conflict involving Iran disrupting global crude flows and tightening supply.

data.title: Australia Economic Data: PPI cools to 0.4% in Q1; Manufacturing PMI beats at 51.3
data.description:
Australia’s final demand Producer Price Index ((PPI)) rose 0.4% quarter-on-quarter in Q1 2026, down from a 0.8% increase in the previous quarter and below market expectations of 0.9%.

 This marked the 23rd consecutive quarter of producer inflation but was the slowest since Q1 2021, with strong demand in the residential property sector contributing to growth.

Separately, the S&P Global Australia Manufacturing PMI was at 51.3 in April 2026, up from flash data of 51.0 and March's 49.8, but this increase was mainly due to longer delivery times caused by the war in the Middle East and international freight delays.

On an annual basis, producer prices increased 3.0% in Q1, slowing from a 3.5% rise in Q4 and marking the softest pace since Q3 of 2021.

The S&P/ASX 200 Index rose 62 points, or 0.7%, to 8,727 in early deals on Friday, the first trading day of May. The Australian dollar held its gains around $0.72 on Friday.

More on Australia:

data.title: Li Auto April deliveries flat year-over-year at 34,085 units; cumulative total hits 1.67M
Dolby forecasts FY2026 revenue of $1.4B-$1.45B while targeting 50-100 bps non-GAAP operating margin improvement
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Dolby Laboratories (DLB) Q2 fiscal 2026

Management view

  • "Revenue and non-GAAP earnings for the quarter came in consistent with the expectations we provided on the call last quarter, and we are maintaining our full year guidance," said President, CEO & Director Kevin Yeaman.
  • Yeaman said Dolby content availability expanded across platforms and formats, including: "Meta has adopted Dolby Vision for content streamed on iOS for both Instagram and Facebook, and Douyin in China has enabled Dolby Vision for content on both iOS and Android," and "Just this quarter, the Super Bowl and the Winter Olympics were available in Dolby Vision and Dolby Atmos."
  • On TVs, Yeaman said, "we're excited about the first Dolby Vision 2 TVs coming to market by the end of this fiscal year," adding, "We expect Dolby Vision 2 to increase ASPs and drive deeper adoption into TV lineups," and pointing to OEM plans from "Hisense, TCL and Philips" plus platform commitments from "Peacock and Canal+."
  • On new revenue streams beyond devices, Yeaman said, "we are beginning to generate revenue from content platforms," highlighting the video distribution program where "Sharp and SK Planet" joined as licensors, and Dolby OptiView wins including "Genius Sports" and "William Hill" using OptiView for horse racing.
  • Senior VP & CFO Robert Park said, "Revenue for the quarter came in at $396 million," and "Non-GAAP earnings per share was $1.37," while also noting Dolby "repurchased $65 million of common stock" and ended with "cash and investments of approximately $675 million."

Outlook

  • Park said, "We are maintaining our full year guidance," and guided FY2026 total revenue to "range from $1.4 billion to $1.45 billion," with licensing revenue "between $1.295 billion and $1.345 billion" and non-GAAP operating expenses "between $780 million and $800 million."
  • Park said, "We continue to expect non-GAAP earnings per share to be between $4.30 and $4.45," and added, "This guidance implies operating margin improvement of between 50 and 100 basis points on a non-GAAP basis."
  • Park reiterated the mix and growth framework: "Dolby Atmos, Dolby Vision and imaging patents" are expected to be "growing roughly 15% and comprising nearly half of our licensing revenue," while "foundational revenue" is expected "to be down slightly."
  • For Q3 FY2026, Park guided revenue to "between $295 million and $325 million" and non-GAAP EPS to "between $0.56 and $0.71," with non-GAAP gross margin "approximately 88%" and non-GAAP operating expenses "between $200 million and $210 million."
  • Versus last quarter’s message of "we're raising our guidance for the year," the current call emphasized continuity, with Yeaman saying results were "consistent with the expectations" and Park saying guidance is being maintained.

Financial results

  • Park reported revenue mix of "Licensing revenue was $372 million" and "products and services revenue was $23 million," and said "We generated approximately $93 million in operating cash flow."
  • Park said GAAP operating expenses included "a $2 million restructuring charge related to actions initiated last year," and that the company declared "a $0.36 dividend, up 9% from our dividend a year ago."
  • On end markets, Park said "Broadcast was up 26% year-over-year due to the large recovery we mentioned on the last call," and "mobile was down 6% year-over-year due to timing of deals," while adding, "We still expect both broadcast and mobile to be up mid-single digits for the full year."

Q&A

  • John Rigatti, Baird: asked about the ramp to consumption-based revenue and the earlier comment that it "should get to about 10% of revenue in the next 3 years"; President, CEO & Director Yeaman: said Dolby is "really pleased with the progress with both Dolby OptiView and the video distribution program," described an NAB preview of a platform that "uses AI to analyze viewer preferences" and said the video distribution program has "40 licensors" and "half a dozen licensees" with expectations "to continue to grow through the year."
  • Rigatti, Baird: asked about "memory pricing" impacts and how it is reflected in guidance; President, CEO & Director Yeaman: said Dolby is "watching that very closely" and that exposure is most visible in "mobile and PC," but "we've not seen a significant impact to our business to date" and "we do have a diverse set of end markets."
  • Patrick Sholl, Barrington: asked whether customers are prioritizing certain device SKUs due to memory prices; President, CEO & Director Yeaman: said OEMs want to "first and foremost, take care of the high end," adding, "we haven't seen a significant impact to date" and noting visibility from "minimum volume commitments."
  • Sholl, Barrington: asked for more detail on automotive penetration and rollout, including China; President, CEO & Director Yeaman: said Dolby is seeing "pretty high penetration" in premium lines and progress "moving deeper into lineups," citing a China launch as "significant" because it is a "4-channel, 8-speaker implementation" aligned with "a mass market car."
  • Ralph Schackart, William Blair: asked timing and implications of the Hyundai 4-channel implementation and expansion outside China; President, CEO & Director Yeaman: said it was "announced very recently" and "I think it was announced at the Beijing Auto Show," adding Dolby will work to expand "into different lines and different geographies" and feels good about "the pipeline."
  • Schackart, William Blair: asked how Douyin and Meta Dolby Vision moves influence mobile OEM conversations; President, CEO & Director Yeaman: said Douyin has "completed rolling out Dolby Vision content across all of Android," and that Meta adoption is "increasing the pipeline for Dolby Vision and Dolby Vision capture across mobile devices."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly negative, concentrating on risk transmission and adoption pacing (e.g., Rigatti referenced "memory pricing" dynamics; Sholl pressed on OEM SKU prioritization and auto penetration).
  • Management tone was slightly positive and measured, repeatedly emphasizing monitoring macro factors while minimizing observed impact: Yeaman said, "we've not seen a significant impact to our business to date," and Park said, "Overall, we are pleased with our performance to date, and things are generally tracking as expected."
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s more upbeat setup ("FY '26 is off to a good start" and "we're raising our guidance for the year"), the current call’s tone was steadier and framed around execution against prior expectations and maintaining guidance.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance posture shifted from Q1’s "we're raising our guidance for the year" to Q2’s "We are maintaining our full year guidance," while keeping the same FY2026 range Park outlined.
  • Q1 highlighted CES-led momentum and early traction (including Roku as the first U.S. streamer in the video distribution pool); Q2 emphasized broader platform and sports availability, more auto wins (BMW, BYD Denza, Lexus, NIO), and nearer-term commercialization signals such as adding licensors ("Sharp and SK Planet") and OptiView customer wins ("Genius Sports" and "William Hill").
  • Analysts’ questioning focus remained consistent on the consumption-based opportunity and macro variables (memory pricing), with Q2 adding deeper probing on how those pressures affect OEM SKU strategies and mass-market auto implementations.

Risks and concerns

  • Yeaman cited macro monitoring areas including "memory pricing, volatility in oil prices," "consumer sentiment readings" and said Dolby updates guidance based on "what we're learning from our customers" and "industry analysts."
  • Park highlighted quarter-to-quarter variability drivers: "the timing of recoveries, minimum volume commitments and true-ups can drive quarterly volatility," and pointed to end-market timing effects in mobile and broadcast.

Final takeaway

Management said Q2 results tracked prior expectations and kept FY2026 guidance intact, while emphasizing expansion catalysts across Dolby Vision adoption in social platforms (Meta and Douyin), upcoming Dolby Vision 2 TV launches by fiscal year-end, and growth initiatives aimed at monetizing content platforms through the video distribution program and Dolby OptiView, alongside continued automotive penetration efforts that include early mass-market-style implementations.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Veeva Systems climbs 9% after S&P 500 addition
data.description:
• Veeva Systems (VEEV) stock price jumped 9% on Friday pre-market hours as it is set to join the S&P 500 (SP500), replacing Coterra Energy (CTRA), with the change effective before market open on May 7, 2026.
  • The reshuffle is tied to Devon Energy (DVN) acquiring Coterra, prompting its removal from the benchmark.
  • The inclusion is significant, as S&P 500 additions often lead to passive fund inflows while index-tracking funds adjust their portfolios to include the new stock.
  • Veeva Systems (VEEV), a cloud software provider focused on the life sciences sector, will join the index under the healthcare segment.
  • The company gets a Seeking Alpha Quant rating of Hold, with the highest factor grades given to profitability.

data.title: Stryker maintains 2026 outlook of 8%-9.5% organic sales growth and $14.90-$15.10 adjusted EPS amid cyber incident recovery
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Stryker (SYK) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "The cyber incident had a big impact on our results" and "resulted in distortions in our first quarter results that will normalize over the course of the year," said (Chairman & CEO Kevin Lobo), adding, "this situation will not enable us to provide the normal level of detail or explanations that you are accustomed to hearing from us."

  • "As of the week of April 1, we were fully operational across our global manufacturing network as we continue to meet demand and support patient care," said (Chairman & CEO Kevin Lobo), after noting the incident "occurred late in the quarter and resulted in a global disruption to our business operations."

  • "For the first quarter, organic sales growth was 2.4%" with "1.9% in the U.S. and 3.9% internationally," said (Chairman & CEO Kevin Lobo), while stating, "we are maintaining our full year guidance" despite the cyber incident impact.

  • "We recently announced the agreement to acquire Amplitude Vascular Systems" and "expect to close in the second quarter," said (Chairman & CEO Kevin Lobo), adding that the acquisition "will help expand treatment options for our peripheral vascular customers" and is "a step toward expanding our presence in the broader cardiovascular space."

  • "At the beginning of Q1, we established our new Ortho Tech business" to "simplify the customer experience, accelerate innovation and increase our speed to market," said (Chairman & CEO Kevin Lobo).

  • "Finally, I'd like to thank Jason for his last 4 years as Vice President of Finance and Investor Relations" and "we look forward to Nick Mead assuming the role of VP and Investor Relations on May 1," said (Chairman & CEO Kevin Lobo).

  • "Our capital order book remains elevated" and "we delivered our best ever Q1 for Mako installations both in the U.S. and internationally," said (VP and Group CFO of MedSurg & Neurotechnology Jason Beach), adding the company expects "sustained momentum from installations and utilization to drive growth in our joint replacement businesses."

  • "Adjusted earnings per share of $2.60" and "adjusted gross margin of 63.6%" were both pressured by the cyber incident, said (VP & CFO Preston Wells), while emphasizing, "we continue to see healthy demand across our businesses."

Outlook

  • "Despite the disruption we experienced this quarter, we are maintaining our full year guidance," said (VP & CFO Preston Wells): "organic net sales growth to be in the range of 8% to 9.5% and adjusted net earnings per share to be in the range of $14.90 to $15.10."

  • "We expect most of the first quarter's lost sales to be realized throughout the rest of the year," said (VP & CFO Wells), and outlined cadence expectations: "catch-up of revenue recognition in Q2" and impacts from "the rescheduling of certain delayed procedures" and "the fulfillment of customer orders impacted by production shutdowns" expected "into the second half of the year."

  • Versus Q4 2025’s setup, management repeated the same full-year ranges: "we expect 2026 organic net sales growth to be in the range of 8% to 9.5% and adjusted net earnings per share to be in the range of $14.90 to $15.10," said (VP & CFO Wells) in the prior quarter.

Financial Results

  • "Organic sales growth was 2.4%," said (VP & CFO Preston Wells), adding, "Pricing had a 0.3% favorable impact, and foreign currency had a 1.6% favorable impact."

  • "Adjusted earnings per share of $2.60 was down $0.24," said (VP & CFO Wells), attributing the decline to "lost manufacturing absorption related to the cyber incident" as well as "tariffs and increased interest expense."

  • "Our adjusted gross margin of 63.6% was 190 basis points lower" and "adjusted operating margin was 21.1% of sales" with "180 basis points" pressure, said (VP & CFO Wells), citing "production shutdowns due to the cyber incident" and "tariffs."

  • "Adjusted other income and expense of $97 million" rose year-over-year and the company still expects "full year 2026 adjusted other income and expense to be approximately $420 million," said (VP & CFO Wells).

  • "Our year-to-date cash from operations was $581 million," said (VP & CFO Wells), and reiterated: "For 2026, we continue to expect our full year effective tax rate to be in the range of 15% to 16%."

Q&A

  • Robert Marcus, JPMorgan: asked for "more specific color" on recovery cadence and whether upside was removed; VP & CFO Wells: said recovery depends on "variability across our business," with some "caught up" later and added, "we're going to really move into those numbers" and reassess after Q2.

  • Larry Biegelsen, Wells Fargo: asked about margin cadence and whether "operating margin" expectations changed; VP & CFO Wells: responded, "from a full year perspective, nothing has changed" and quarter-to-quarter cadence "might change slightly" due in part to tariffs timing.

  • Joanne Wuensch, Citi: asked about orthopaedics competition; Chairman & CEO Lobo: said, "we love our position as market leaders in robotics" and cited launches including "Mako Shoulder" mid-year and said guidance assumes "outgrow the Orthopaedic marketplace by 200 to 300 basis points."

  • Ryan Zimmerman, BTIG: asked if there was a "pause in orders" vs inability to serve; Chairman & CEO Lobo: said, "we did not see a pause in orders" but "definitely had a pause in shipments" due to being unable "to make product for almost 3 weeks."

  • Travis Steed, BofA: asked if AVS suggests more risk-taking and earlier-stage deals; Chairman & CEO Lobo: said it is "more related to the type of business than the size of our company" and "we feel very confident about" AVS.

  • David Roman, Goldman Sachs: asked for Inari update; Chairman & CEO Lobo: said attrition and "Strykerize the sales force" is "mostly behind us now" and described expectations for "clear sailing" and continued innovation.

  • Samantha Kurtz, Piper Sandler: asked cyber impacts by segment; VP & CFO Wells: said orthopaedics had consigned inventory so procedures continued but "revenue recognition" lagged, while MedSurg capital items will skew recovery later; Chairman & CEO Lobo added, "Endo and Medical are more capital-intensive."

  • Elaine Cui, Raymond James: asked about Smart Hospital; Chairman & CEO Lobo: said SmartCare combines "Vocera and the care.ai businesses" and provides "seamless integration" with "Secure II beds," adding, "I'm very bullish on this for the long term."

  • Shagun Singh Chadha, RBC: asked for more granular quarterly modeling and lessons learned; VP & CFO Wells: said, "We don't guide to the quarters" and declined to split deferred procedures vs production delays; Chairman & CEO Lobo said, "the recovery of our backups, 100% of those worked" and "we're still very, very focused on recovery."

  • Michael Kratky, Leerink: asked about GLP-1 and AVS-driven cardio expansion; Chairman & CEO Lobo: said, "We're not seeing any GLP-1 impact" and added he would not predict whether future deals become "a platform deal."

  • Caitlin Cronin, Canaccord: asked Foot & Ankle and Incompass ankle; Chairman & CEO Lobo: said, "We have launched the Incompass Total Ankle" but "the PROPHECY guides have not yet been approved" and expects it to "take off" once approved.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing on visibility, with repeated efforts to quantify cyber impacts and quarterly cadence, including "how much is deferred procedures versus production delays" (Shagun Singh Chadha, RBC).

  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks but more guarded on granularity, highlighted by "we continue to see healthy demand" (VP & CFO Preston Wells) alongside "I will not be going into detailed sales results by business" (VP & CFO Wells).

  • Versus Q4 2025’s more upbeat framing ("Our 2025 results were outstanding" (Chairman & CEO Kevin Lobo)), Q1 2026 introduced a more constrained posture on disclosure ("will not enable us to provide the normal level of detail" (Chairman & CEO Lobo)).

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2026 centered on operational disruption and recovery cadence ("The cyber incident had a big impact on our results" (Chairman & CEO Kevin Lobo)), while Q4 2025 emphasized broad-based momentum and multi-year margin expansion ("Our 2025 results were outstanding" (Chairman & CEO Lobo)).

  • Guidance ranges were repeated quarter to quarter, with Q1 2026 reiterating the same full-year framework amid disruption ("we are maintaining our full year guidance" (Chairman & CEO Lobo)), while Q4 2025 positioned guidance off "strong momentum entering 2026" (Chairman & CEO Lobo).

  • Analyst focus shifted from growth drivers, pricing, and capital environment in Q4 2025 to cyber-driven modeling uncertainty and recovery mechanics in Q1 2026, with multiple questions on sales and margin cadence tied to the incident.

Risks and Concerns

  • "The cyber incident occurred towards the end of the quarter" and drove "delays in revenue recognition" and "delayed shipments," said (VP & CFO Preston Wells), with recovery varying by business depending on "operating models" and "consignment structures."

  • Tariffs remained a margin headwind: Q1 results reflected tariffs and management said guidance includes "some anticipated improvements in the tariff outlook," according to (VP & CFO Wells).

  • Geopolitical exposure was cited as incremental risk: "The conflict in Iran has had a modest effect" and "persistent geopolitical risks" remain, said (VP & CFO Wells), while also pointing to long-term opportunity in the region.

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as materially distorted by a late-quarter cyber incident, limiting business-level detail while keeping full-year targets intact. Executives maintained that demand, procedural volumes, and the capital order book remained healthy, and positioned recovery as a mix of Q2 revenue-recognition catch-up with a heavier second-half normalization for made-to-order and rescheduled activity. Alongside recovery execution, management highlighted the pending Amplitude Vascular Systems acquisition (expected to close in Q2), internal reporting/segment changes tied to the new Ortho Tech business, and continued product momentum led by record Q1 Mako installations and upcoming launches including Mako Shoulder on Mako 4 mid-year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: NIO reports April deliveries rise 22.8% Y/Y to 29,356 units; cumulative total hits 1.11M
